answers.intake_q1: When did the elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on the elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: What activities or injuries, if any, preceded the onset of your elbow pain?
answers.intake_a3: I was playing tennis when the pain began.
answers.intake_q4: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your arm or hand?
answers.intake_q5: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q6: Have you tried any treatments or medications for the elbow pain, and if so, what was the effect?
answers.intake_a6: I iced it the first day and it helped with the pain
answers.intake_q7: Does the pain worsen with gripping or lifting activities, such as holding a tennis racket or turning a doorknob?
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms?
answers.intake_summary: A patient presents with 3 weeks of lateral elbow pain that started while playing tennis, worsens with lifting, and improves with ice. There are no neurological symptoms, swelling, redness, or systemic signs.

note.soap:
S: The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began while playing tennis and worsens with lifting objects but improves with ice. She denies any numbness, tingling, weakness, swelling, redness, warmth, fever, or systemic symptoms.

O: Phone evaluation only; no physical examination or vital signs available.

A: The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by lateral elbow pain exacerbated by gripping and lifting activities following tennis play. 
Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy. Patient is not pregnant.

P: 
 - First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen at recommended doses. 
 - Alternatives: Topical NSAID gels applied over the affected area. 
 - Medication safety considerations: Avoid exceeding recommended NSAID doses to prevent gastrointestinal, cardiovascular, liver, and kidney adverse effects. Use caution in patients with underlying renal or liver disease (not present here). 
 - Return precautions: Return if pain worsens, if there is new onset of numbness, tingling, weakness, swelling, redness, or systemic symptoms such as fever. 
 - Testing: Not indicated at this time given typical presentation; consider imaging or nerve conduction studies if symptoms persist or neurological signs develop.

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.title: content.txt
evidence.snippet: Check if you have tennis elbow The main symptom of tennis elbow is pain on the outside of your elbow. The pain may range from mild discomfort when you move your arm to constant pain that can affect your sleep. It may be worse when: - lifting or bending your arm - gripping objects - moving your wrist Other symptoms of tennis elbow include: - tenderness or swe…

evidence.title: Tendinitis: MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: onnect muscles to bones. They help your muscles move your bones. Tendinitis is the severe swelling of a tendon. Tendinitis usually happens after repeated injury to an area such as the wrist or ankle. It causes pain and soreness around a joint. Some common forms of tendinitis are named after the sports that increase their risk. They include tennis elbow, golf…

evidence.title: Elbow Injuries | Elbow Disorders | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…
